title: Shortest path exactly K edges
description: Shortest path with exactly K edges

examples.explanation: Start at node 0 with remaining=2. Path 0->1 (weight 10), remaining=1. From node 1, go to 2 (weight 1), remaining=0, at node 2 ≠ dest, discard. Backtrack. Path 0->2 (weight 3), remaining=1. From node 2, go to 1 (weight 8), remaining=0, at node 1 = dest, total weight=11. Only valid path with exactly 2 edges to destination 1 is 0->2->1 with weight 11. Minimum valid path weight = 11.

Start at node 0, remaining=3. Path 0->1 (5), remaining=2. From 1->2 (4), remaining=1. From 2->3 (3), remaining=0, at dest, total=5+4+3=12. Path 0->2 (10), remaining=2. From 2->3 (3), remaining=1, need exactly 0 edges, so invalid. Other paths: 0->1->2->3 is only valid path. Minimum=12.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(V^K)
approaches.summary: Explore all possible paths of exactly K edges from the source to the destination using depth-first search, keeping track of the minimum path weight. This is straightforward but impractical for large graphs due to exponential growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Shortest Path with Exactly K Edges via DFS
ladders.desc: Find the minimum weight path from source to destination using exactly K edges by exploring all possible paths.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the shortest path between two nodes in a weighted directed graph, but with the constraint that the path must contain exactly K edges. The brute force DFS approach systematically explores all possible paths of length K from the source to the destination. The intuition is that by traversing every possible sequence of K edges starting from the source, we can guarantee we'll find the optimal path if it exists. We use DFS because it naturally explores all branches of the search tree. The algorithm starts at the source node with remaining edges = K. At each step, we recursively visit all neighbors, decrementing the remaining edges count. When remaining edges reaches 0, we check if we're at the destination. We maintain a global minimum variable that gets updated whenever we find a valid path. The time complexity is O(V^K) because in the worst case, each node can have up to V neighbors, and we explore paths of depth K. Space complexity is O(K) for the recursion stack. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete DFS traversal that uses the recursive exploration subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling cycles properly (which is fine here since we track edge count), forgetting to reset visited nodes (we don't need to track visited since we want all K-edge paths), and not handling the case where no path exists. The real code pattern involves a recursive DFS function that explores neighbors until K edges are used.

examples.trace: Start at node 0 with remaining=2. Path 0->1 (weight 10), remaining=1. From node 1, go to 2 (weight 1), remaining=0, at node 2 ≠ dest, discard. Backtrack. Path 0->2 (weight 3), remaining=1. From node 2, go to 1 (weight 8), remaining=0, at node 1 = dest, total weight=11. Only valid path with exactly 2 edges to destination 1 is 0->2->1 with weight 11. Minimum valid path weight = 11.

Start at node 0, remaining=3. Path 0->1 (5), remaining=2. From 1->2 (4), remaining=1. From 2->3 (3), remaining=0, at dest, total=5+4+3=12. Path 0->2 (10), remaining=2. From 2->3 (3), remaining=1, need exactly 0 edges, so invalid. Other paths: 0->1->2->3 is only valid path. Minimum=12.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive DFS Path Exploration
ladders.desc: Implement DFS to explore all paths of a given length from a starting node.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach: a recursive function that explores all paths of exactly remaining edges from a current node. The intuition is that to find paths of length K, we need to explore all possible sequences of edges. At each recursive call, we have a current node and remaining edges count. If remaining edges is 0, we check if we're at the destination and return the path weight if valid. Otherwise, we iterate through all neighbors of the current node, recursively call the function with the neighbor as the new current node and remaining-1, and add the edge weight to the result. We keep track of the minimum weight across all recursive branches. The time complexity for this subroutine on a graph with V vertices is O(V^K) in worst case because each call branches to up to V neighbors, and depth is K. Space is O(K) for recursion depth. This connects to L0 by being the main function called to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include not handling the base case correctly (remaining=0), not accumulating weights properly, and not exploring all neighbors. The real code pattern is a recursive function dfs(node, remaining) that returns the minimum weight from node to destination using exactly remaining edges.

examples.trace: dfs(0,2): remaining=2≠0. Neighbors: to 1 (weight 2), call dfs(1,1) +2; to 2 (weight 5), call dfs(2,1)+5. dfs(1,1): remaining=1≠0. Neighbors: to 3 (weight 1), call dfs(3,0)+1. dfs(3,0): remaining=0, at dest, return 0. So dfs(3,0)=0, dfs(1,1)=0+1=1, dfs(0,2) from path 0->1->3 = 1+2=3. dfs(2,1): remaining=1, no neighbors, return -1. So min(3, -1) = 3.

dfs(0,2): remaining=2. To 1 (weight 1), call dfs(1,1)+1. dfs(1,1): to 0 (weight 2), call dfs(0,0)+2. dfs(0,0): remaining=0, at dest, return 0. So dfs(0,0)=0, dfs(1,1)=0+2=2, dfs(0,2)=2+1=3. No other paths. Output=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Exploring All Paths of Fixed Length
ladders.desc: Understand how to systematically generate all paths with exactly K edges in a graph.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that to find the shortest path with exactly K edges, we must consider every possible sequence of K edges from the source. This is essentially generating all paths of length K in the graph. The intuition is that without any optimizations, the only way to guarantee finding the minimum weight path is to examine all possibilities. We can think of this as a tree where each node represents a graph vertex, and each level of the tree corresponds to taking one edge. The root is the source, and at depth K, we look for leaves that are the destination. We need to traverse this entire tree (pruning only when remaining edges hit 0). The algorithm involves starting at the source, and at each step, branching to all neighbors, decrementing a counter for edges used. When the counter reaches 0, we check if current node is destination. We accumulate edge weights along each branch. The time complexity is exponential: O(V^K) because each of the K levels can branch to up to V neighbors. Space is O(K) for the recursion stack. This connects to L1 as the conceptual foundation for the recursive exploration. Common mistakes include not considering all neighbors at each step, or incorrectly counting edges. The real code pattern is iterative or recursive branching at each node.

examples.input: Graph: 0->1, 0->2, 1->3, 2->3. Generate all paths from 0 to 3 with exactly 2 edges
examples.trace: Start at 0, edges left=2. Branch to neighbor 1: now at 1, edges left=1. From 1, branch to neighbor 3: edges left=0, at node 3, path valid: 0->1->3. Backtrack to 0. Branch to neighbor 2: at 2, edges left=1. From 2, branch to neighbor 3: edges left=0, at 3, path valid: 0->2->3. All paths generated.

examples.input: Graph: 0->1, 1->0. Generate all paths from 0 to 0 with exactly 2 edges
examples.trace: Start at 0, edges left=2. Branch to 1: at 1, edges left=1. From 1, branch to 0: edges left=0, at 0, path valid: 0->1->0. Done.

testCases.input: Graph: 0->1. Generate paths from 0 to 1 with 1 edge
Graph: 0->1, 1->2. Generate paths from 0 to 2 with 2 edges
Graph: 0->1, 0->2. Generate paths from 0 to 2 with 1 edge
Graph: 0->1, 1->0. Generate paths from 0 to 0 with 2 edges
Graph: 0->1, 1->2, 2->3. Generate paths from 0 to 3 with 3 edges
Graph: 0->1, 1->2, 0->2. Generate paths from 0 to 2 with 2 edges
Graph: 0->1, 1->2, 2->0. Generate paths from 0 to 0 with 3 edges
Graph: 0->1, 1->2, 2->1. Generate paths from 0 to 1 with 2 edges
Graph: 0->1, 1->0, 0->2. Generate paths from 0 to 2 with 2 edges
Graph: 0->1, 1->2, 2->3, 3->4. Generate paths from 0 to 4 with 4 edges

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with Edge Counting
ladders.desc: Traverse a graph while keeping track of how many edges have been traversed.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves walking through a graph (via DFS or BFS) but with an additional constraint: we only want to traverse exactly a specified number of edges. The intuition is that in many path-finding problems, we need to limit the depth or length of traversal. Here, we combine standard graph traversal with a counter that decrements with each edge taken. When the counter hits zero, we stop traversing further from that node. This is different from standard DFS which might traverse until all nodes are visited or a destination is found without edge limit. The algorithm: start at a source node with a given edge limit L. Use a stack (for DFS) or queue (for BFS) to store (node, edges_used). While not empty, pop an element. If edges_used == L, process the node (e.g., check if it's destination). Otherwise, for each neighbor, push (neighbor, edges_used+1). Time complexity depends on graph structure and L; in worst case O(V^L). Space is O(V) for storage. This connects to L2 as the mechanism to generate paths of fixed length. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in edge counting, or not stopping when edge limit is reached. The real code pattern uses a loop or recursion with an edge counter.

examples.trace: Start at (0,0). Pop (0,0): edges_used=0<2. Push neighbors: (1,1), (2,1). Pop (1,1): edges_used=1<2. Push neighbor: (3,2). Pop (2,1): edges_used=1<2, no neighbors. Pop (3,2): edges_used=2==2, process node 3. Done.
Start (0,0). Pop (0,0): push (1,1). Pop (1,1): push (0,2). Pop (0,2): push (1,3). Pop (1,3): edges_used=3, process node 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Graph Neighbors
ladders.desc: Access and process all adjacent nodes from a given vertex in a graph.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves, given a vertex in a graph, retrieving all vertices that are directly connected by an edge (neighbors). In the context of the shortest path problem, this is crucial because at each step of traversal, we need to consider all possible next nodes to move to. The intuition is that graphs represent connections, and to explore paths, we must examine each connection from the current node. For a directed graph, neighbors are nodes reachable via outgoing edges; for undirected, all adjacent nodes. The algorithm depends on graph representation: if using an adjacency list, we simply iterate through the list for the given vertex; if using an adjacency matrix, we scan the row for non-zero entries. Time complexity is O(degree(v)) for adjacency list, O(V) for matrix. Space is O(1) for the iteration. This connects to L3 as the core action in graph traversal: at each node, we iterate neighbors to continue the traversal. Common mistakes include not handling cases where a vertex has no neighbors (empty list), or confusing incoming/outgoing edges. The real code pattern is a for-loop over graph[vertex] for adjacency list.

examples.trace: For vertex 0, access graph[0] which is list [1,2]. Iterate: first neighbor 1, then neighbor 2. Done.
For vertex 1, graph[1] is [0]. Iterate: neighbor 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Understand how to represent a graph using lists of neighbors for each vertex.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: representing a graph in code so we can work with it. A graph consists of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between nodes). For the shortest path problem, we need a way to store which vertices are connected and the weights of edges. The adjacency list representation is common: for each vertex, we maintain a list of its neighbors. In a weighted graph, each neighbor entry includes the neighbor vertex and the edge weight. The intuition is that this representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs (O(V+E)) and allows quick access to all neighbors of a vertex. For example, if vertex 0 has edges to vertices 1 and 2 with weights 5 and 3, the adjacency list for vertex 0 is [(1,5), (2,3)]. The algorithm to create this: initialize a dictionary or array of lists. For each edge from u to v with weight w, append (v,w) to the list for u. Time to build is O(E). Space is O(V+E). This connects to L4 because once we have the graph represented, iterating over neighbors is straightforward. Common mistakes include not handling directed vs undirected graphs (for undirected, add edges in both directions), or forgetting to store weights. The real code pattern in Python: graph = defaultdict(list) and for each edge, graph[u].append((v,w)).

examples.trace: Start with empty dict. Add edge 0->1: append (1,10) to list for key 0. Add edge 0->2: append (2,20) to list for key 0. Vertices 1 and 2 have no outgoing edges, so empty lists.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(K * V^2)
approaches.summary: Use dynamic programming where dp[u][k] stores the shortest path from source to node u using exactly k edges, computed recursively with memoization to avoid redundant calculations. This approach leverages overlapping subproblems to improve over brute force.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Shortest Path with Exactly K Edges using DP Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding shortest path from source to target using exactly K edges with DP memoization.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the shortest path from a source node to a target node using exactly K edges in a weighted directed graph. The brute force approach would explore all possible paths of length K, which is exponential. We use dynamic programming with memoization to store intermediate results and avoid redundant calculations. The key insight is that the shortest path to node u using exactly k edges can be expressed in terms of shortest paths to its neighbors using k-1 edges. We define dp[u][k] as the minimum cost to reach node u from source using exactly k edges. We initialize dp[source][0] = 0 (zero cost to reach source with 0 edges) and all others as infinity. For each k from 1 to K, we compute dp[v][k] = min over all edges (u,v) of (dp[u][k-1] + weight(u,v)). We use memoization to store computed dp values to avoid recomputation. The final answer is dp[target][K]. Time complexity is O(K * V^2) in worst case (dense graph) as for each k we might check all possible edges. Space complexity is O(K * V) for the DP table. Common mistakes include not handling infinity properly (use a large number), forgetting to check for unreachable states, and incorrect base case initialization. This approach efficiently solves the problem by breaking it into overlapping subproblems.

examples.input: Graph: 3 nodes (0,1,2), edges: 0->1 weight 5, 0->2 weight 3, 1->2 weight 1, source=0, target=2, K=2
examples.trace: Graph: nodes 0,1,2; edges 0→1(5), 0→2(3), 1→2(1); source=0, target=2, K=2

examples.input: Graph: 4 nodes, edges: 0->1 weight 2, 1->2 weight 3, 2->3 weight 4, 0->2 weight 10, source=0, target=3, K=3

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP State Computation with Memoization
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that computes dp[u][k] using memoization to avoid redundant calculations.
ladders.explanation: The core of the DP approach is a function compute(u, k) that returns the shortest path cost from source to node u using exactly k edges. We use memoization (top-down DP) to store results in a 2D array memo[u][k]. The base cases: if k == 0, return 0 if u == source else INF. For k > 0, we compute the minimum over all incoming edges (v->u) of (compute(v, k-1) + weight(v,u)). If no valid path exists, return INF. We initialize memo with -1 (uncomputed) and store computed values to avoid recomputation. Time complexity for each call is O(V) in worst case (checking all possible predecessors), but with memoization overall O(K*V^2). Space is O(K*V) for memo table. This sub-routine directly feeds into the full problem by providing the DP value needed. Common mistakes include not handling cycles correctly (the recursion might loop infinitely, but since k decreases, it's safe), and incorrect base case for k=0. The intuition is that to reach u with k edges, we must come from some neighbor v with k-1 edges.

examples.input: Graph: 3 nodes, edges: 0->1 weight 5, 0->2 weight 3, 1->2 weight 1, source=0, compute(2,2)
examples.trace: Call compute(2,2). k=2>0, check incoming edges: from 1: compute(1,1)+1, from 0: compute(0,1)+? (no edge 0->2 for incoming). compute(1,1): k=1>0, incoming from 0: compute(0,0)+5=0+5=5, memo[1][1]=5. So compute(2,2)=min(5+1=6, INF)=6.

examples.input: Graph: 2 nodes, edge: 0->1 weight 7, source=0, compute(1,2)
examples.trace: Call compute(1,2). k=2>0, incoming from 0: compute(0,1)+7. compute(0,1): k=1>0, incoming edges to 0? none, so INF. So compute(1,2)=INF.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP State Transition for K Edges
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the DP recurrence relation: dp[u][k] = min over edges (v->u) of (dp[v][k-1] + weight(v,u)).
ladders.explanation: The central insight is the recurrence relation that defines the DP state. Let dp[u][k] be the minimum cost to reach node u from source using exactly k edges. For k = 0, dp[source][0] = 0 and dp[u][0] = INF for u != source. For k > 0, dp[u][k] = min over all edges (v,u) in the graph of (dp[v][k-1] + weight(v,u)). This works because to reach u with k edges, the last edge must be from some predecessor v, and the path to v must use exactly k-1 edges. We take the minimum over all possible v. This recurrence breaks the problem into smaller subproblems. We can compute this iteratively for k from 1 to K. Time complexity per k is O(E) if we iterate over edges, but in worst case O(V^2) for dense graphs. Space is O(K*V). This logic is the heart of the DP approach and is used in the sub-routine. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing (mixing up u and v), not initializing dp properly, and forgetting to handle cases where no edge exists. The intuition is dynamic programming optimal substructure: the optimal path to u with k edges consists of an optimal path to some v with k-1 edges plus the edge from v to u.

examples.input: Graph: 3 nodes, edges: 0->1 weight 5, 0->2 weight 3, 1->2 weight 1, dp values for k-1: dp[0][1]=INF, dp[1][1]=5, dp[2][1]=3, compute dp[2][2]
examples.trace: dp[2][2] = min over incoming edges to 2: from 1: dp[1][1]+1=5+1=6, from 0: dp[0][1]+? (no edge 0->2 as incoming, so skip). So min is 6.

examples.input: Graph: 2 nodes, edge: 0->1 weight 7, dp values for k-1: dp[0][1]=INF, dp[1][1]=7, compute dp[1][2]
examples.trace: dp[1][2] = min over incoming edges to 1: from 0: dp[0][1]+7=INF+7=INF. So INF.

dp[0][0]=0, dp[1][0]=INF, edge 1->0 weight 5, compute dp[0][1]
dp[0][1]=3, dp[1][1]=4, edges: 0->2 weight 2, 1->2 weight 1, compute dp[2][2]

dp[0][0]=0, dp[1][0]=INF, edges: 0->1 weight 10, compute dp[1][1]

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and Edge Iteration
ladders.desc: Learn to represent a weighted directed graph and iterate over incoming/outgoing edges for DP transitions.
ladders.explanation: To implement the DP recurrence, we need an efficient way to represent the graph and access edges. For a weighted directed graph with V nodes, common representations are adjacency list or adjacency matrix. For this DP, we often need to iterate over incoming edges to a node u (i.e., edges v->u) to compute dp[u][k]. We can store an adjacency list where for each node u, we keep a list of (v, weight) for edges v->u (reverse adjacency) or store outgoing edges and iterate over all nodes v to check if edge v->u exists. Using an adjacency matrix weight[v][u] allows O(1) edge lookup but O(V^2) iteration. For sparse graphs, adjacency list is better. We'll use a list of edges or a dictionary. The key operation is: for each node u and each k, loop over all possible predecessor nodes v and if edge v->u exists, compute dp[v][k-1] + weight(v,u). Time to iterate over edges is O(E) per k, but in worst case O(V^2). This building block is essential for the core logic. Common mistakes include incorrect graph representation (directed vs undirected), not handling missing edges (use INF weight), and inefficient iteration. The intuition is that we need to explore all possible ways to reach u.

examples.input: Graph: 3 nodes, edges: 0->1 weight 5, 0->2 weight 3, 1->2 weight 1, find all incoming edges to node 2
examples.trace: Node 2 has incoming edges from node 1 with weight 1 and from node 0 with weight 3. So list is [(1,1), (0,3)].

examples.input: Graph: 2 nodes, edge: 0->1 weight 7, find all incoming edges to node 0
examples.trace: Node 0 has no incoming edges, so empty list.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Minimum Cost Update with Infinity Handling
ladders.desc: Implement the operation to compute the minimum of values, correctly handling infinity (unreachable states).
ladders.explanation: In the DP recurrence, we frequently need to compute the minimum over several values, some of which might be infinity (representing unreachable states). We define INF as a very large number (e.g., 10**9) that acts as a sentinel for unreachable. The operation is: given a current value 'current' and a new candidate 'candidate', update current = min(current, candidate). If candidate is INF, it should not affect the minimum. If current is INF and candidate is finite, current becomes candidate. If both are INF, result remains INF. We must ensure INF is large enough that INF + weight does not overflow or become finite, but in practice we check if candidate is INF before adding. In code, we can initialize dp values as INF and update using min. For example, to compute dp[u][k], we start with INF, then for each incoming edge (v,u), if dp[v][k-1] != INF, compute new_cost = dp[v][k-1] + weight, and update dp[u][k] = min(dp[u][k], new_cost). This operation is fundamental to the DP update step. Common mistakes include using a small INF that gets exceeded by actual paths, or not checking for INF before addition. The intuition is that we want the smallest finite cost, ignoring unreachable states.

examples.input: current = INF, candidates: [5, INF, 3], compute min
examples.trace: Start with current = INF. Compare with 5: min(INF,5)=5, current=5. Compare with INF: min(5,INF)=5. Compare with 3: min(5,3)=3. Final 3.
Start with current=7. Compare with INF: min(7,INF)=7. Compare with INF: min(7,INF)=7. Final 7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding DP State and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Learn the basic idea of dynamic programming state definition and base case initialization.
ladders.explanation: Dynamic programming (DP) is a method to solve problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid recomputation. A DP state is a way to represent a subproblem. For this problem, we define state dp[u][k] as the minimum cost to reach node u from source using exactly k edges. The base cases are the simplest subproblems that can be solved directly. Here, if k = 0, we are at the start: dp[source][0] = 0 (cost 0 to be at source with 0 edges), and for any other node u != source, dp[u][0] = INF (impossible to be at u with 0 edges unless u is source). We initialize a 2D array of size V x (K+1) with INF, then set dp[source][0] = 0. This initialization is crucial because it defines the starting point for the DP recurrence. The intuition is that DP builds up solutions from these base cases. For example, to compute dp[u][1], we use dp[v][0] for predecessors v. Common mistakes include incorrect base case (e.g., setting dp[u][0] = 0 for all u), or not using a large enough INF. This foundation is needed for all higher levels.

examples.input: V=3, source=0, K=2, initialize dp base cases
examples.trace: Create dp array 3x3 all INF. Set dp[0][0] = 0 because at source with 0 edges cost 0. All other dp[u][0] for u=1,2 remain INF.

examples.input: V=2, source=1, K=1, initialize dp base cases
examples.trace: dp array 2x2 all INF. Set dp[1][0] = 0 since source is node 1. dp[0][0] remains INF.

approaches.name: Matrix Exponentiation O(V^3 * log K)
approaches.summary: Represent the graph as an adjacency matrix and use matrix exponentiation to compute the shortest paths for exactly K edges by treating it as a min-plus matrix multiplication problem. This is efficient for large K but requires dense matrix operations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Shortest Path with Exactly K Edges using Matrix Exponentiation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of matrix exponentiation with min-plus multiplication to find shortest path with exactly K edges.
ladders.explanation: This solution solves the problem of finding the shortest path with exactly K edges between any two nodes in a weighted directed graph. The core idea is to represent the graph as an adjacency matrix where matrix[i][j] = weight of edge from i to j (or INF if no edge). We then use matrix exponentiation with min-plus multiplication (also called tropical matrix multiplication) to compute the shortest paths for exactly K edges. The min-plus multiplication of two matrices A and B produces matrix C where C[i][j] = min_k(A[i][k] + B[k][j]). This corresponds to combining paths: if A represents paths of length L and B represents paths of length M, then C represents paths of length L+M. By exponentiating the adjacency matrix to the K-th power using binary exponentiation, we get a matrix where result[i][j] = shortest path from i to j using exactly K edges. The intuition is that matrix multiplication in min-plus algebra corresponds to path concatenation, and exponentiation corresponds to repeating this process K times. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize adjacency matrix with edge weights (INF for missing edges, 0 on diagonal for self-loops with 0 weight). 2. Define min-plus multiplication: result[i][j] = min_k(A[i][k] + B[k][j]). 3. Use binary exponentiation: start with identity matrix (0 on diagonal, INF elsewhere), multiply by adjacency matrix when bit is set. 4. After K exponentiations, query result[src][dest]. Time: O(V³ log K) from matrix multiplication (V³) repeated log K times. Space: O(V²) for matrices. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into a complete solution. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle INF correctly in min-plus operations, incorrect identity matrix initialization, off-by-one errors in exponentiation. Real code pattern: Use nested loops for matrix multiplication with min() and addition, careful INF handling (use large number like 10**9).

examples.trace: Adjacency matrix A: [[0,2,10],[INF,0,3],[INF,INF,0]] (INF=10^9). Compute A^2 using min-plus: result[0][2] = min(A[0][0]+A[0][2]=0+10=10, A[0][1]+A[1][2]=2+3=5, A[0][2]+A[2][2]=10+0=10) = 5. So shortest path with exactly 2 edges: 0→1→2 with cost 2+3=5.
A^3[0][3] = min over k (A[0][k] + A²[k][3]) = 3 (path 0→1→2→3 with cost 1+1+1).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Min-Plus Matrix Multiplication
ladders.desc: Implement the min-plus (tropical) matrix multiplication used in the exponentiation.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine performs min-plus matrix multiplication, which is the fundamental operation in the matrix exponentiation approach. Given two matrices A and B of dimensions n×n, we compute matrix C where C[i][j] = min over k of (A[i][k] + B[k][j]). This differs from standard matrix multiplication where we use sum of products; here we use min of sums. The intuition is that if A represents shortest paths of length L and B represents shortest paths of length M, then C represents shortest paths of length L+M by concatenating paths: to go from i to j in L+M steps, we go from i to some intermediate node k in L steps (cost A[i][k]), then from k to j in M steps (cost B[k][j]), and we take the minimum over all possible k. This is essentially a dynamic programming step for path concatenation. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize result matrix with INF. 2. For each i, j, k: result[i][j] = min(result[i][j], A[i][k] + B[k][j]). 3. Handle INF carefully: if A[i][k] or B[k][j] is INF, skip as it represents unreachable. Time: O(n³) from triple nested loops. Space: O(n²) for result matrix. Connection: This multiplication is used repeatedly in the binary exponentiation at L0. Common mistakes: incorrect initialization (not setting to INF), not handling INF in addition (INF + anything should remain INF), using wrong loop order. Real code pattern: Use three nested loops, initialize result with INF, update with min().

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Exponentiation with Matrices
ladders.desc: Use binary exponentiation to compute matrix power efficiently.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on computing M^K efficiently using binary exponentiation, adapted for matrices with min-plus multiplication. The core insight is that instead of multiplying matrix M by itself K times (O(KV³)), we can use exponentiation by squaring to achieve O(V³ log K). The intuition is similar to computing numeric exponents: M^K can be computed by repeatedly squaring M and multiplying intermediate results based on the binary representation of K. For example, to compute M^13 (binary 1101), we compute M^1, M^2, M^4, M^8 and multiply M^8 * M^4 * M^1. In min-plus context, multiplication is min-plus multiplication. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize result as identity matrix (0 on diagonal, INF elsewhere). 2. Let current = M. 3. While K > 0: if K is odd, result = multiply(result, current). Then current = multiply(current, current), and K = K // 2. 4. Final result is M^K. Time: O(V³ log K) as multiplication is O(V³) and done O(log K) times. Space: O(V²) for matrices. Connection: This is the algorithmic framework that uses the min-plus multiplication from L1 to solve the full problem at L0. Common mistakes: using wrong identity matrix (should be min-plus identity: 0 on diagonal, INF elsewhere), not updating current correctly, off-by-one in exponentiation. Real code pattern: While loop on K, with if (K & 1): result = mul(result, current), then current = mul(current, current), K >>= 1.

examples.trace: Binary exponentiation: K=3 (binary 11). Start result=I=[[0,INF],[INF,0]], current=M. K odd: result=mul(I,M)=[[0,1],[INF,0]]. current=mul(M,M)=[[0,1],[INF,0]] (since M^2[0][1]=min(0+1,1+0)=1). K=1: K odd: result=mul(result,current)=mul([[0,1],[INF,0]],[[0,1],[INF,0]])=[[0,1],[INF,0]]. current=mul(current,current)=same. K=0: done. So M^3=[[0,1],[INF,0]].

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation as Adjacency Matrix
ladders.desc: Convert a graph with weighted edges into an adjacency matrix suitable for min-plus operations.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves representing a directed weighted graph as an adjacency matrix, which is the input format for the matrix exponentiation approach. For a graph with V vertices, we create a V×V matrix where entry matrix[i][j] = weight of edge from node i to node j. If there is no edge, we set it to INF (a large number representing infinity). Additionally, we set matrix[i][i] = 0 to represent a path of zero cost from a node to itself (important for identity matrix and self-loops). The intuition is that this matrix encodes all possible one-step paths: from i to j in exactly one edge costs matrix[i][j]. This representation allows us to use matrix operations to combine paths: multiplying matrices corresponds to concatenating paths. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize matrix with INF everywhere. 2. Set diagonal to 0. 3. For each edge (u, v, w): matrix[u][v] = min(matrix[u][v], w) if multiple edges. Time: O(V²) to initialize, plus O(E) to fill edges. Space: O(V²). Connection: This matrix is the base matrix M that we exponentiate at higher levels. Common mistakes: forgetting to initialize with INF, not setting diagonal to 0, handling multiple edges incorrectly (should take min weight). Real code pattern: Create 2D list with INF, set diagonal, iterate edges to update.

examples.trace: Initialize 3x3 matrix with INF. Set diagonal to 0. Add edges: matrix[0][1]=2, matrix[0][2]=10, matrix[1][2]=3. Result: [[0,2,10],[INF,0,3],[INF,INF,0]].
Initialize 4x4 INF matrix, set diagonal to 0. Add edges: matrix[0][1]=1, matrix[1][2]=1, matrix[2][3]=1. All other entries remain INF.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Min-Plus Addition and Comparison
ladders.desc: Perform the fundamental min and addition operations with INF handling.
ladders.explanation: This foundational operation involves computing min(a, b) and a + b where a and b can be real numbers or INF (infinity), which represents unreachable or infinite cost. In the context of shortest paths, INF is a sentinel value (e.g., a very large number like 10**9) that indicates no path exists. The key rules are: 1. min(x, INF) = x for any finite x, min(INF, INF) = INF. 2. x + INF = INF for any x (including INF). This ensures that if any part of a path is unreachable, the entire path is unreachable. The intuition is that when combining path costs, we want to ignore impossible paths and only consider feasible ones. For example, in min-plus multiplication, we compute min over sums: if A[i][k] is INF, then A[i][k] + B[k][j] is INF and doesn't affect the min. Step-by-step: To compute min(a, b): if a is INF, return b; if b is INF, return a; else return smaller of a and b. To compute a + b: if a is INF or b is INF, return INF; else return a + b. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection: These operations are used in every step of min-plus matrix multiplication at L1. Common mistakes: not defining INF consistently, incorrect handling of INF in addition (e.g., INF + 5 should be INF, not a large number). Real code pattern: Define INF = 10**9, use if statements or min() with care.

examples.trace: min(5, INF): since 5 is finite and INF is larger, min returns 5. add(5, INF): since one operand is INF, result is INF.
min(INF, INF): both are INF, so result is INF. add(3, 4): both finite, so 3+4=7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Matrix Representation and Basic Operations
ladders.desc: Understand matrices as 2D arrays and perform basic operations like access and initialization.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding matrices as two-dimensional arrays (lists of lists in Python) and performing basic operations such as creating a matrix, accessing elements, and initializing with values. A matrix of size n×n is represented as a list of n rows, each row being a list of n elements. For example, a 2x2 matrix can be [[a, b], [c, d]]. The intuition is that matrices provide a compact way to represent pairwise relationships, which in graphs correspond to edges between vertices. Step-by-step: 1. Create matrix: Use nested loops or list comprehension, e.g., [[0]*n for _ in range(n)]. 2. Access element: matrix[i][j] gives element at row i, column j. 3. Initialize: Set all elements to a value, e.g., INF or 0. 4. Set diagonal: matrix[i][i] = 0 for all i. Time: O(n²) to initialize. Space: O(n²). Connection: This basic structure is used to represent the adjacency matrix at L3. Common mistakes: using [[0]*n]*n which creates shallow copies (all rows reference same list), off-by-one in indices. Real code pattern: Use list comprehension for safe initialization.

examples.trace: Use list comprehension: [[5 for _ in range(3)] for _ in range(3)]. This creates 3 rows, each with 3 elements of value 5.

examples.input: Access element at row 1, column 2 in matrix [[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]]
examples.trace: Matrix indices start at 0. Row 1 is [4,5,6], column 2 is the third element (index 2), which is 6.

testCases.input: Set diagonal of 3x3 INF matrix to 0
Set element at row 1, column 1 of [[0,0],[0,0]] to 9
Create 3x3 matrix with row i, column j = i+j
